Harvard University
Digital Accessibility Services
Digital Accessibility Services
Digital Accessibility Services
Digital Accessibility Services
Data Visualizations, Charts, and Graphs
Data visualizations such as charts, diagrams, and infographics are popular as they can present data in a concise and compact way.
But visualizations are often not accessible due to low contrast colors, insufficient labels, or images without alternative text.
This means that part of your audience might be excluded from the information being presented. With some thoughtful planning, we can create charts and graphs that are accessible to everyone.
In this page:
 Design and Color Choices
 Labels and Descriptions
 Supplemental Formats
 Building your Data Visualization
Design
Person looks at pie chart with various colored slices.
When you’re deciding what kind of chart or graph you want to create, it might be tempting to try something different or unique. But when it comes to presenting data, visualizations are most powerful and accessible when the design is familiar rather than a complex novelty.
The important thing is to not overwhelm people with information. If you try to show too much, people might lose the most important pieces. Instead, carefully select only the data that will support your clarity of intent, so that your main message isn’t lost.
Keep your designs simple and clear, and avoid animations as they can be distracting and disorienting. If you must include some type of movement in the visualization, provide a way for people to turn it off.
Color Choices
Color can be a powerful visual tool in visualizations. When designing a chart or graph, be mindful of the colors you use, the contrast of the text against the background, and how you’re using color to convey meaning. For adjacent data parts, like bars or pie wedges, use a solid border color to separate and add an additional layer of visual distinction between the pieces.
color bars showing range of color options.
Text Contrast
The color of any text should have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against the background color.
You can measure the color contrast with a tool such as the WebAIM Contrast Checker.
Object Contrast
For bars in a bar graph or sections of a pie chart, aim to provide a high degree of contrast against the background and against each element. The contrast ratio for these should be 3:1. For example, if you’re using red and blue to show two bars in a bar graph, the red and blue should have a contrast of at least 3:1.
Conveying Meaning
If color is the only way that you’re conveying meaning, your information might be lost to someone who can’t see color. Instead of relying on color alone to explain the data, add an additional visual indicator, such as a pattern, shape, or text label so that even if someone is unable to perceive color, they’ll still be able to understand the data. But be careful not to overdo patterns - keep them simple and clear.
Example 1 - Shape Differences
2021 Crop Soybean Cash Market Statistics (February 7 – February 11, 2022) - a line graph indicating the daily maximum, minimum, and average prices.
The colored lines also have shape differences (diamond, square, triangle) to identify them.
Credit: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture
Example 2 - Pattern Differences
Bar graph with two data points distinguished by striped and dotted patterns.
The columns have different patterns to help distinguish beyond just color.
Image Credit: Zach Grosser - Accessible Colors for Data Visualization
Labels and Descriptions
Whether a data visualization is simple or complex, there should be clear text that labels the significant parts of the data. Ensure major elements are identified, such as the chart title, the horizontal and vertical axis and other notable framework elements. Use labels and legends that clearly mark and distinguish the data points.
Also add either brief alternative “alt” text or a longer description that provides context and explains the most important details about the visualization.
Example 3 - Direct Labeling
lables for colored lines are placed directly next to each line.
If possible, use "direct labeling" - position the label directly beside or adjacent to the data point. Here with have line graph with the text for each line directly adjacent. 
Image Credit: storytelling with data.
Example 4 - Data Point Callouts
Pie chart of favorite foods with labels next to each slice - tacos 59%, pizza 34%, salad 7%.
Each data point has the callout for the amount so a user doesn't have to guess or rely on color to identify different slices.
Consider Providing a Supplemental Format
Another way to make sure your data and message can be understood by your audience is to provide a supplemental format for the data.
People learn in different ways - some might be visual learners while others prefer a more analytical text-based approach. Presenting data in multiple formats will ensure that people can digest the information in the way that best suits their needs.
Example 5 - Include a table or spreadsheet
Here we provide both the bar chart and a link to the data in table form.
Bar Chart of First and Second Year Student Weekly Activities.
Detail: See full data for Student Weekly Activities
Example 6 - Chart Description
Consider providing a link to a longer description or include it adjacent to the chart in a collapsible section.
Bar Chart of First and Second Year Student Weekly Activities.
Weekly Activities for 1st and 2nd Year Students measured in hours.
 Social Events - First Year 6 hours, Second Year 2 hours.
 Volunteer Activities - First Year 4 hours, Second Year 2 hours.
 Coursework - First Year 9 hours, Second Year 9 hours.
Building your Data Visualization
Before you get started, it’s worth pausing and thinking about the data you want to present and what platform, program, or tool you'll use to build your visualization. We'll share three options below for how you might build and display your data visualization.
1. Image with Alt Text
2. In Documents
3. Chart Library or Custom Build
1. Visualization built anywhere, presented as Image with Alt Text
Icon of an image.
No matter where you create your data visualizations, you will likely have an option to export as an image (such as .jpg or .svg) or to take a screenshot. These image files may then be used anywhere that you’d like to share the data. When sharing as an image, be sure to include alternative text that succinctly describes the visualization.
When writing alt text consider the type of the chart and the intent of it's use in your content. Try to summarize key points. For example:
 A bar graph of fundraising by mayoral candidate. Candidate Smith has raised twice as much as Candidate Jones.
 A pie chart of television programming where sports is 32%, news 48%, and sitcoms are 30%.
Advantages
The color and design of the visualization is stable and will not change if imported from one program to another.
Disadvantages
Best for simple visualizations with limited content. Images may become pixelated. Content owner will have to keep track of the source file for future edits and changes.
2. Visualizations created and presented in Documents
Icon of document with text.
Microsoft, Google, and other common document creation programs offer tools for creating visualizations as part of your standard document editing. Learn more at Microsoft Chart Guidance or Google Chart Guidance.
Advantages
These programs have a low learning curve for users new to designing visualization. You can easily make accessible diagrams and charts.
Disadvantages
If sharing in a different program or slide theme, the colors or text may change, introducing accessibility errors. In some cases your chart may be converted into an image that would then require alt text.
3. Visualizations created using a Chart Library or Custom Build
Icon of a screen with a gear overlaid.
You may choose to design your own visualizations or use a chart library to design and embed visualizations. Whichever you choose, there are a few additional considerations to keep in mind, such as keyboard access, screen reader access, focus indicator, and names and roles. However, some well-designed chart libraries (such as Highcharts) take care of many of these considerations for you.
Advantages
Allows the greatest flexibility in design. Versatility in embedding the data visualization in a variety of places for display.
Disadvantages
Steeper learning curve. Some tools are not accessible, so use the criteria above to evaluate before building a chart or graph. You might also have to pay for a license to use the tool.
Additional Resources
 Technique: Describing graphs
 10 Guidelines for DataViz Accessibility – Highcharts
 An intro to designing accessible data visualizations by Sarah L. Fossheim
 5 easy ways to make your data visualization more accessible
 Accessibility Considerations in Data Visualization Design
 How to make charts and graphs more accessible – Pope Tech

Top 10 Essential Types of Data Visualization of 2026: From Basic to Advanced
Top 10 Essential Types of Data Visualization of 2026: From Basic to Advanced
Posted by: Nathan Misa
TL;DR
From bar charts to heatmaps to waterfalls, data visualizations reveal patterns and trends at a glance. Choosing the right chart type ensures clarity, accuracy, and stronger communication. Great data storytelling depends on visuals that match the audience’s needs and context. Yellowfin provides every core visualization plus advanced options, so teams can explore data their way.  
Try Yellowfin free
Data visualization helps people comprehend and attain insight into big data. It represents complex data in visually interesting ways that assist in our understanding, and paves the way for a greater link between the provided raw data, and our overall engagement with it.
Nowadays, we accumulate data in ever-increasing sizes. We need an intelligent way to understand vast volumes of information. In analytics, we often use different types of data visualization tools to convey complex data. Do you ever wonder how useful it really is?
In this blog, we cover the top 10 essential types of data visualization you need to have in your solution today, and which general use cases they best fit - with illustrative examples.
What are the prevalent techniques of data visualization?
Firstly, data visualization helps businesses dive deeper into data exploration, analyze hypotheses, and communicate results effectively. Moreover, it helps people detect patterns, catch trends, and find correlations in data that numbers alone can't convey.
Are you overwhelmed by a vast landscape of data visualizations and unsure of how to choose the best data visualization for reporting? We will help you by listing their details. You can also read our guide on how to choose the right chart type for good data visualization if you're further along your journey.
Let us take you on a whirlwind tour of popular data visualization techniques.
1) Line Graph
Line graph data visualization example
Line graph data visualization example
An example of a line graph, created in Yellowfin.
A line graph demonstrates the values of different categories over time. Specifically, it shows changes in value across continuous measurements of items. It illustrates an overall trend leaving no room for confusion. That's why people use it for several business use cases.
Overall trends and different types of graphs help business leaders forecast projections for future outcomes. When the line moves up, it often shows positive changes. On the other hand, the movement of the line going down shows negative changes. It proves handy when you explicitly want to show trends for multiple categories over the same course of time.
When to use line charts?
2) Column Chart
Column chart data visualization example
Column chart data visualization example
An example of a column chart, created in Yellowfin.
One data visualization technique we frequently see is a column chart. People use it to compare different values side by side. Using it is a great idea when you want to pay attention to total figures instead of the shape of a trend.
A column chart is quite popular as it is simple to understand and can compare diverse kinds of data. It often shows the time on the horizontal axis, while the vertical axis displays values. Note that a column and line chart combination is a good choice when showing figures and an overall trend.
When to use column charts?
3) Bar Graph
Bar chart data visualization example
Bar chart data visualization example
An example of two comparative bar graphs, created in Yellowfin.
Another data visualization method is a bar graph, also called bar chart, that indicates the values by the length of the bar. The other axis, meanwhile, shows the categories that are supposed to be compared. We can draw a bar chart both vertically and horizontally. Horizontal bar charts are a good choice when plotting multiple bars.
At one glance, a bar chart helps us contrast data sets from several groups while exhibiting the relationship between two axes. Different types of data visualization bar charts also show changes in data over time.
When to use bar charts?
4) Pie Chart
Candlestick chart data visualization exampleCandlestick chart data visualization example
Candlestick chart data visualization example
Candlestick chart data visualization example
Another common data visualization technique is pie charts. As a circular graph, it shows data of relative sizes through pie slices. It serves various application purposes, including showing percentages of customer types, product revenues, and country profits. It is simple to grasp, and for this reason, people employ it to demonstrate relative sizes.
Pie charts work well to display percentages as they show each element as part of a whole. The whole pie, nevertheless, shows one hundred percent of the total. The pie slices symbolize different parts of the pie chart. However, it is not a good choice when you want to display complex information for a thorough explanation. In this case, you need to pick up some other types of data visualization charts.
When to use pie charts?
5) Funnel Chart
Funnel chart data visualization example
Funnel chart data visualization example
An example of a funnel chart, created in Yellowfin.
Amongst different types of data visualization charts, a funnel chart is considered the best while working with multiple business contexts. It helps track users in a pipeline flow; for example, for sales, it specifically shows the decreasing values as customers go through the sales funnel.
The funnel width displays the number of users that make their way at each step. It shows a linear process comprising sequential stages and a swift picture of where people drop out of the process.
When to use funnel charts?
6) Map-based Plot

Map-based plot is another type of data visualization technique that helps show geographically related data. It is a useful method when you want to plot a dataset that corresponds to actual geographic locations. Instead of plotting values, it shows value by filling regions with color on a map.
Its data expression is crystal clear, intuitive, and presents data in the form of maps. Readers can read the distribution of data in each region, so it brings convenience to make better decisions. The aesthetic element is another significant reason to use it. It transforms boring content into eye-catching content when you equip it with an aesthetically-appealing map.
When to use map-based plots?
7) Heat Map/Heat Grid
Heat map data visualization example
Heat map data visualization example
An example of a heatmap, created in Yellowfin.
Another type of data visualization graph that has been widely talked about is a heat map, also known as a heat grid. The heat map displays values on two variables of interest. While the axis variable can turn out to be categorical or numeric, the grid comes into shape by splitting each variable into several levels. It shows differences in data in the form of color variations.
The values of grid cells on heat grids are colored, with darker colors often indicating higher values.  Colors help communicate values to the viewer so they can identify trends quicker. Hence, interpreting a heatmap is easy.
When to use heat map?
8) Waterfall Chart
Waterfall chart data visualization example
Waterfall chart data visualization example
An example of a waterfall chart, created in Yellowfin.
A waterfall chart visually shows the overall growth or decline in value between two specified points. Its goal is to show how a value has risen or declined over time. Amongst all types of data visualization tools, this one is considered best for understanding the final outcome.
It dis-aggregates and visualizes different distinctive components that contribute to the net change instead of reflecting starting and ending values in two bars.
When to use waterfall chart?
9) Scatter Plot
Scatter plot data visualization example
Scatter plot data visualization example
An example of a scatter plot, created in Yellowfin.
Another data visualization technique is a scatter plot. There are plenty of data visualization examples that show the versatility of scatter plots in terms of visuals and use cases. Here, we will discuss an example with circles to showcase how data is portrayed in this type of technique. While the circle color presents categories of data, the circle size represents the volume of the data. We represent the data for two variables by points against the vertical and horizontal axis.
The purpose of a scatter plot is to show the relationship between the provided variables, which, in turn, helps identify trends or correlations in data. The usefulness of scatter plots emerges when the data is significantly large, as identification of trends is possible only in the presence of extensive data points.
When to use scatter plot?
10) Pictogram Chart
Regarding data visualizations, a pictogram is another type that uses icons and images to represent data. It presents simple data aesthetically engagingly, using repeated icons to show simple data.
Apart from making the data engaging, it also proves handy in situations when cultural differences emerge as a hurdle to making the audience understand the data. Remember that a pictogram is not a good choice for large data sets as it becomes difficult to count.
When to use pictogram chart?
What are other options for data visualization?
Data visualization is clearly a powerful tool that may help you become a better communicator in your reports and business intelligence dashboards.
Although the techniques discussed above are some of the most popular, there are many more types of data visualization available to use.
Other methods of visualizing information include:
Correlation Matrix
A correlation matrix is a grid showcasing relationships between multiple variables. Each cell in the matrix represents the correlation coefficient, often visualized with a color gradient. It's a popular choice amongst other data visualization types for analyzing patterns and relationships. It is one of the most ideal methods for identifying positive or negative correlations in complex datasets.
Bubble Charts
Bubble chart type of data visualizationBubble chart type of data visualization
Bubble chart type of data visualization
Bubble chart type of data visualization
An example of a bubble chart, created in Yellowfin.
Bubble charts are scatter plots enhanced with bubbles of varying sizes to represent additional data dimensions. These types of graphs are great for comparing three variables at once. Their ability to convey relationships, trends, and proportions in a visually engaging way surely puts them on top amongst others. Some vendors like Yellowfin also offer GIS bubble maps which visualize geographic information system point data in the same style as bubble charts.
Cartograms
Cartograms are unique visualizations that distort geographic areas to visually represent the proportions of data, such as population density or GDP. They effectively guide viewers to focus on the relative magnitude of data across different regions by prioritizing data representation over accurate geographic representation.
Circle Views
Circle views utilize circular representations to depict data points, frequently arranged in clusters or hierarchical structures. This visualization method excels at portraying proportions and relationships within datasets. As a data visualization technique, circle views offer versatility and visual appeal, effectively presenting complex information in a concise and easily understandable format.
Network Diagrams
Network diagrams display connections between nodes (points) and edges (lines). It is an excellent method for mapping relationships in social networks, computer systems, or communication paths. Network diagrams stand out among different types of graphs for their ability to simplify complex interconnected systems.
Dendrograms
Dendrograms are tree-like diagrams that visually represent hierarchical relationships between items. These visualizations are frequently employed in fields such as clustering analysis, biology, and genealogy. They serve as prime examples of how graphs can effectively reveal nested structures within datasets.
Dot Distribution Maps
Dot distribution maps utilize dots to visually represent various phenomena' presence, density, or frequency across a geographical area. These maps excel at highlighting spatial patterns and distributions. One can simplify complex geographic data by employing visual symbols, making it easier to understand and interpret.
Open-High-Low-Close Charts (Candlestick Chart)
Candlestick chart data visualization exampleCandlestick chart data visualization example
Candlestick chart data visualization example
Candlestick chart data visualization example
An example of a candlestick chart, created in Yellowfin.
These financial charts, commonly known as candlestick charts, are indispensable for visualizing stock market data. They effectively depict a security's opening, highest, lowest, and closing prices within a specific timeframe. Among various data visualization techniques, candlestick charts are a cornerstone of financial analysis and are a classic example of the diverse types used in the business world.
Word Clouds
Word clouds visualize text data by varying the size of words based on frequency or importance. These types of data visualization are perfect for identifying key themes in large text datasets. As one of the more artistic types, word clouds make data interpretation engaging and easy.
Polar Areas
Polar area charts utilize a circular layout to represent data proportions, similar to pie charts, but with a stronger emphasis on comparisons within categories. This visualization method is particularly effective for showcasing cyclical data trends. Polar area charts offer an innovative approach to data visualization, providing a unique and visually engaging perspective on data relationships.
Radial Trees
Radial trees are circular representations of hierarchical tree diagrams, offering a visually striking and compact alternative to traditional linear formats. These visualizations are particularly effective for displaying family trees, organizational structures, and taxonomic classifications.
Ring Charts
Ring charts, or donut charts, are pie charts with a hollow center. These types of graphs are excellent for showcasing proportions and comparisons. As a popular type of data visualization, ring charts effectively simplify complex datasets into digestible visuals.
Choropleth Maps
Choropleth maps use color shading to represent data across geographic regions. They are commonly used for demographic, economic, or political data. With the help of choropleth maps, businesses can showcase unmatched spatial data trends, aiding them to make smarter decisions.
Sankey Diagrams
Sankey diagrams display flows and relationships between entities, with line thickness proportional to the data magnitude. These types of data visualization diagrams are excellent for energy flow, budget analysis, and process mapping. Sankey diagrams are standout examples of different types of graphs for showcasing transitions.
Span Charts
Span charts effectively visualize data variability by displaying ranges or fluctuations over time. These charts are widely used in fields such as finance and environmental science to analyze trends and identify patterns. Among various data visualization techniques, span charts excel at highlighting temporal variations and identifying significant changes within datasets.
Streamgraphs
Streamgraphs are variations of stacked area charts where layers flow smoothly to represent data streams over time. They are perfect for showing changes in proportions and creating visually captivating and informative graphs.
Bullet Graphs
Bullet graphs are compact bar charts designed to compare performance against a target. They are widely used in business dashboards and are often chosen the most among other types of data visualization graphs. They are concise and highly effective for summarizing key metrics.
Treemaps
Treemaps utilize a hierarchical structure of nested rectangles to visualize the proportions of different categories within a dataset. This visualization method is particularly effective for representing large and complex datasets with multiple levels of categorization. Treemaps are a widely used data visualization technique for presenting proportional relationships in various fields, including business and research.
Wedge Stack Graphs
Wedge stack graphs utilize stacked wedges arranged in a circular format to visualize layered data. This visualization method effectively represents cumulative data proportions. Compared to standard graph types, wedge stack graphs offer a visually engaging and creative approach to data presentation.
Violin Plots
Violin plots visualize data distribution and density, combining box plots and density plots. These are excellent for comparing distributions across multiple categories. Violin plots are a versatile type of data visualization for statistical analysis.
Highlight Tables
Highlight tables enhance data comparison by employing color gradients within a tabular format to emphasize specific data points. This visualization method effectively compares numerical values, simplifying complex data sets more effectively than many other graph types. Visually highlighting trends and highlighting tables make it easier to identify patterns and insights.
Timeline
Timelines are visual representations that depict events or data points in chronological order. They are ideal for visualizing historical occurrences, project milestones, and any sequence of events. As a fundamental data visualization technique, timelines effectively illustrate temporal relationships.
Ultimately, the support for these various types of visualizations come down to the business intelligence (BI) and analytics solution you adopt, with certain modern vendors offering extensive data visualization tools - including Yellowfin.
Best enterprise data visualization tool for charting
While other solutions in the market have simple visualization options, Yellowfin provides a wide range of different types of data visualization and customization, with over 50 types of charts. In addition, it provides a flexible design canvas for novices and expert designers alike. Unlike the limited functionality offered by solutions in the market, Yellowfin provides a complete package.
Thankfully, white-labeling and integration with native apps is also possible. It also incorporates location intelligence, such as in-built mapping ability and Google Maps API for visuals. Furthermore, it carries innovative charts & supports JavaScript charting libraries that include D3 and three.js.
Learn more about Yellowfin's data visualization tools and product suite and check out how our BI solution works for various types of data visualization. It also supports them out-of-the-box for corporate and product teams.
Conclusion
Data visualization is an indispensable tool for simplifying complex datasets, uncovering patterns, and enabling informed decision-making. From line graphs that highlight trends over time to heat maps that reveal intensity through color gradients, each visualization type serves unique purposes in analytics. By understanding when and how to use different types of graphs, your business can communicate insights more effectively, spot opportunities, and drive strategic actions.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
What are the four types of data visualization?
The four main types of data visualization graphs are bar charts, line charts, scatter plots  and heat maps. Each type is tailored to specific data analysis goals. Understanding these types helps create impactful and relevant visualizations.
What are the 4 levels of visualization?
The four levels of visualization include descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive visualizations. Descriptive shows what happened, diagnostic explains why it happened, predictive forecasts future trends, and prescriptive suggests solutions. Each level builds on the previous, offering deeper data insights.
What are the data visualization field's key objectives?
The key objectives are simplifying complex data, identifying trends and patterns, enhancing decision-making, and effectively communicating insights. Different types of data visualization types turn raw data into intuitive visuals, helping users understand information quickly. It bridges the gap between data analysis and actionable insights.
What are the 5 steps in data visualization?
The 5 steps in data visualization are defining objectives, collecting data, choosing the right visualization type, designing the visual, and interpreting results. This process ensures the visualizations are accurate, engaging, and aligned with the intended message. Each step is crucial for impactful data communication.
Next steps: Discover best data visualization tools with Yellowfin
See for yourself how Yellowfin offers stunning  data visualization capability to use when building interactive dashboards, data stories and presentations.

Data visualization is the act of representing data and information with visual components like charts, maps, or graphs. Visually depicting data allows Data Analysts to find trends, patterns, or outliers the data contains and to share these findings with others within an organization. An important aspect of receiving professional data visualization training to set yourself up for a career change is learning what kinds of fields and industries that training will help you find work in. Each industry has different requirements and expectations for prospective employees, and you should consider these expectations when you start your training. This is true for students who have an idea of what kind of work they want to do and for students who aren’t yet sure what path they want to take after learning data visualization skills.
What is Data Visualization?
Data visualization uses graphics like plots, charts, or animations to represent data findings. These visual depictions communicate complicated data relationships in an accessible and engaging format to an organization’s internal and external stakeholders, even for those who don’t come from a technical background.
Data visualization has applications across industries and professions, from data analytics to business intelligence. The data visualization process is typically broken into four goals: to generate ideas, to illustrate concepts, to provide avenues into visual discovery, and to convey data in the form of visualizations.
Read more about what data visualization is and why you should learn it.
What Can You Do with Data Visualization Skills?
Data visualization provides a concise and interactive way to depict data trends. Visualizations incorporate design techniques like color and contrast so audience members can access information that may otherwise be difficult to comprehend. Data visualization can provide a better understanding of a business’s operations and how activities connect. It offers insights into important business metrics that can lead to smoother daily operations and successful project execution. Data visualization conveys specific insights into customers’ preferences, demographics, and shopping patterns. The information is presented in an easy-to-understand format, and the audience is taken on a journey that leads to a data-driven conclusion.
Common Industries That Use Data Visualization
Data visualization is used in a variety of professional settings and industries. Those who work in healthcare, finance, retail, and hospitality are just some of the professionals who work with visualizations to convey important data findings. They use tools such as Tableau and Excel to analyze, visualize, and share their data findings with others. The following sections will briefly examine how data professionals working in each of these industries use data visualization.
Healthcare
Healthcare analytics is a broad term for any activities that result from analyzing and visualizing data pertaining to costs and claims, clinical information, patient behavior, and pharmaceutical information. The average salary in the U.S. for a Healthcare Analyst is $65,000. Various members of the healthcare community use data visualization for different purposes. When COVID-19 began to spread, the healthcare community had to work quickly to address the health crisis and work to ensure the health risks didn’t become greater. Data visualization helped them perform tasks like charting the spread of the virus, analyzing patient demographics, monitoring various healthcare facilities, identifying risk areas and help centers, and evaluating how accessible pharmacies were.
Healthcare analytics also has a range of applications beyond COVID-19-related tasks. It can help healthcare professionals identify patients at high risk for chronic illness, which benefits not only the patient but the practitioner and healthcare provider. It can be used to help with clinical research, which improves clinical guidelines and trials. With the help of predictive modeling, healthcare professionals can stay abreast of potential risks and treatment outcomes for patients. Predictive analytics also has large-scale applications such as predicting the occurrence of outbreaks and providing information about possible ways to prevent future occurrences.
Machine learning algorithms help healthcare professionals spot anomalies in scans such as differences between MRI images. These algorithms allow them to do so over a thousand times faster than could be done manually. Healthcare analytics also is an effective method for evaluating healthcare professionals. After patients write reviews about a doctor they’ve seen, the information is gathered and analyzed to improve the patient experience. Even concerns such as staffing issues can be helped with data visualization. If a hospital is understaffed, patient care suffers, whereas if it’s overstaffed, the hospital may find itself wasting money. Healthcare analytics involves reviewing the past months’ or years’ admission records to offer predictions on what the upcoming weeks may look like regarding admission rates. This helps hospitals anticipate patient volumes so they can schedule staff accordingly.
Retail
Another industry that benefits from the data visualization process is retail. Both local and regional companies use data visualization to gain insight into customer behavior to better anticipate their customers’ wants and needs and provide products accordingly. These tools can also provide insights into the anticipated fluctuations in customer demand in the coming months and years.
Retail Store Managers earn approximately $70,000 a year. Data visualization allows these professionals, as well as others who work in the retail sector, to study customer demographics and income so they can locate wealthy and inhabited areas in which they can grow their businesses. Finding new locations for enterprises can help a company improve its supply chain, better its current performance, and pinpoint any aspects of its business where profit and costs don’t line up. Because of how competitive the retail industry is, it’s imperative for stakeholders to stay on top of retail flow from manufacturing to distribution.
As more retailers see the benefit of working with customer data, they can continue to reap additional benefits from the insights provided by the numbers. In retail, business teams are commonly treated like disparate entities. This can make it difficult for entire organizations because it means that some teams may not have access to whole data, whereas others do. With the help of data analytics and visualization, retail professionals can bring teams together so that each team has full access to necessary end-to-end data and can make better decisions based on this knowledge. Data visualization provides visual evidence of any slow-downs or weaknesses in the chain that must be addressed.
Finance
Those who work with financial analytics rely on data analytics and visualization for a range of tasks. Finance teams collect huge volumes of data so their organization can use it to notice industry trends, establish feasible goals, perform more efficiently, and offer better services and products. However, simply collecting and processing information isn’t enough for a Financial Analyst; these professionals have to also provide actionable insights to their audience so they can understand the information being presented. Financial data visualization allows them to present statistics and data insights to stakeholders in a way they can access.
Financial Analysts work with data visualization so their teams can translate complicated data into smaller, easier-to-understand chunks. It also allows them to replace static figures with narratives and stories that provide a context for the numbers being depicted. Data visualization allows these professionals to convey financial performance to other members of their organization, as well as to share insights with different teams, so that various members of an organization are kept up-to-speed on any indicators that may be dropping such as the profit-margin threshold. By using graphics like maps and charts, thousands of rows of data from multiple sources can be expressed on one screen, making it much easier for a finance team to understand them. Financial dashboards can then be created to visually compare and analyze key performance indicators. This provides real-time insights into financial data.
Financial Analysts are compensated well for their contributions to their organizations. These professionals make an average yearly salary of $95,000. Their job outlook is strong over the next decade, with a projected 9% growth rate, which is above the national average.
Hospitality
Those in the hospitality industry must quickly find and analyze data to remain competitive. Data visualization allows them to condense huge volumes of data into visual components like charts and graphs. This allows Hoteliers to identify industry trends, as well as to offer predictions about what may transpire in the near future so that they can base their decision on this information.
Hotel data originates from many internal and external sources such as website traffic, social media, and airline data. These various information sources must be consolidated by Hoteliers through data visualization to be of use. Visually depicting data allows these professionals to easily spot any trends or relationships between data points. Visualization also helps them spot any strengths and weaknesses. Insights such as the knowledge that people are booking rooms because of a hotel’s location help Hoteliers identify how they can better market to potential customers and ultimately improve their bottom line.
Hotels that keep track of occupancy rates can make accurate forecasts about room demand, giving them an edge over the competition. This information also allows them to price rooms appropriately. In addition, the data insights conveyed through visualizations can illustrate the differences between what hotels pay in labor costs and what they bring in profit-wise. Customer experience also benefits from data visualization. Customers who have a good experience at a hotel often share their feedback through surveys or on social media. Visualizing guest data pertaining to their length of stay or how much they spent on amenities, Hoteliers can use these insights to categorize customers and provide them with specialized offers.
Hoteliers earn an average yearly salary of $60,000. Their field is expected to grow 18% over the next decade, which is well above the national average.
Learn Data Visualization Skills with Noble Desktop
Noble Desktop has in-person and live online data visualization training. Tableau for Data Visualization Bootcamp instructs students on how to use Tableau Public’s visualization tools. Noble’s Data Analytics Certificate teaches core business intelligence, statistics, data analysis, and data visualization concepts.

The Python for Data Science Bootcamp provides instruction on how to use statistics to make machine learning models. Noble’s Excel Bootcamp teaches fundamental spreadsheet functions, including macros, formulas, and PivotTables. Those interested in working with SQL can enroll in Noble’s SQL Bootcamp.

Data Visualization in 2026: The Complete Beginner's Guide to Turning Data into Business Insights
Data Visualization in 2026: The Complete Beginner's Guide to Turning Data into Business Insights
Doris Ratego
Doris Ratego
Funlings Entertainment•4K followers
Funlings Entertainment•4K followers
Every day, businesses generates thousands of data points, sales figures, customer interactions, inventory levels, website clicks. Imagine trying to make sense of a spreadsheet with 10,000 rows of numbers. Now imagine seeing that same information as an interactive dashboard that reveals patterns at a glance. That's the transformative power of data visualization.
As we navigate through 2026, data visualization has evolved from a nice-to-have skill to an absolute business necessity. It is the difference between reading a complex recipe in a foreign language versus watching a step-by-step cooking video. Both contain identical information, but one speaks directly to how our brains process information.
This guide walks you through everything you need to know about data visualization, from its fundamental importance to creating your first professional chart using Python. Whether you are a business owner seeking competitive advantage or a professional looking to add a powerful skill to your toolkit, this journey starts here.
What Is Data Visualization?
Data visualization is the art and science of transforming raw numbers into meaningful visual stories. It is the bridge between complex datasets and human understanding, turning columns of figures into charts, graphs, dashboards, and interactive displays that our brains can process almost instantly.
Here is the truth: the human brain processes visual information 60,000 times faster than text. A bar chart comparing quarterly sales instantly shows which quarter performed best. Getting that insight from a table of numbers takes significantly longer and requires far more mental effort.
Why Data Visualization Matters: The Business Case You Can't Ignore
Picture a marketing team managing five campaigns across social media, email, and paid ads. Without visualization, they are drowning in CSV files, trying to mentally compare click-through rates, conversion percentages, and cost-per-acquisition across platforms. With visualization, a single dashboard instantly reveals which campaign is winning, which platform delivers the best ROI, and where budget should be reallocated, all in seconds.
The Speed Advantage
In today's marketplace, speed is not just an advantage; it's survival. When your competitor can spot a market trend on Monday and pivot their strategy by Tuesday, you cannot afford to spend a week analyzing spreadsheets. Data visualization collapses the time between question and answer, insight and action.
The Communication Breakthrough
Here is where data visualization becomes truly powerful: it democratizes data across your organization. Your finance team, marketing department, operations manager, and executive leadership can all look at the same visual dashboard and extract relevant insights, no technical degree required.
Pattern Recognition
The most exciting benefit of data visualization is its ability to reveal patterns that human eyes simply cannot detect in numerical form. Correlations, trends, outliers, and anomalies jump out from well-designed visualizations like neon signs in the dark.
A manufacturing company might discover that machine breakdowns correlate with specific humidity levels, an insight buried in thousands of sensor readings but immediately visible in a scatter plot. An e-commerce business might notice that customers who buy product A almost always purchase product B within 30 days, a cross-selling opportunity revealed through visualization of purchase patterns.
The Cost of Invisibility
Now, what happens when businesses do not embrace data visualization? The costs are real and measurable:
Missed Opportunities: That emerging market trend your data captured three months ago? Your competitor saw it in their dashboard and acted. You are just discovering it now in quarterly reports.
Slow Response Times: When problems require days of analysis to identify, small issues become expensive crises.
Poor Resource Allocation: Without clear visual comparisons, businesses invest based on gut feeling rather than data-driven evidence, often pouring money into underperforming channels while starving their best opportunities.
Team Frustration: When insights remain locked in complex datasets, talented team members cannot access the information they need to make smart decisions, leading to disengagement and inefficiency.
Real-World Applications: Where Data Visualization Creates Impact
Let me illustrate how data visualization transforms operations across different sectors, with examples you can relate to regardless of your industry.
Finance and Banking: Security in Real-Time
Imagine a bank processing millions of transactions daily. Traditional fraud detection meant reviewing flagged transactions in batch reports, often hours after suspicious activity occurred. Modern visualization dashboards display transaction patterns in real-time, using color-coded alerts and geographic heat maps.
When an unusual pattern emerges, say, a card being used in three different countries within an hour, it lights up the dashboard like a fire alarm. Security teams can respond in seconds, not hours. The visualization does not just show that something is wrong; it shows exactly what, where, and how severe the threat is.
Healthcare: Saving Lives Through Clarity
In hospitals, patient outcome dashboards transform how medical teams make decisions. Instead of flipping through pages of lab results and vital signs, doctors see visual timelines showing how a patient's condition is trending. A sudden change in the pattern,a spike or dip triggers immediate attention.
During the pandemic, healthcare administrators used visualization dashboards to track bed capacity, ventilator availability, and staff allocation across multiple facilities. These visual tools helped them make life-or-death decisions about resource distribution in real-time.
Retail and E-Commerce: Understanding Your Customer
A retail business owner once told me they were spending thousands on marketing with disappointing results. When we visualized their customer journey data, the problem became instantly clear: they were losing 70% of potential customers at the checkout page due to unexpected shipping costs.
That single visualization, a funnel chart showing where customers dropped off, led to a simple fix (displaying shipping costs earlier in the process) that increased conversions by 45%. The data was always there, but without visualization, the critical bottleneck remained invisible.
Manufacturing: Efficiency at Scale
Smart factories use real-time visualization to monitor hundreds of machines simultaneously. Dashboard displays show production rates, energy consumption, and maintenance needs using intuitive gauges and color-coded alerts.
When a machine's efficiency drops below optimal levels, the visualization system doesn't just report a number; it displays a visual comparison against normal performance, triggers an alert, and even suggests predictive maintenance before breakdown occurs. This prevents costly downtime and keeps production flowing smoothly.
Tools Transforming Data Visualization in 2026
As we progress through 2026, emerging trends are reshaping how we visualize data. Understanding these trends helps you choose the right tools for your needs.
The AI Revolution
Artificial intelligence is no longer a futuristic concept, it is embedded in today's leading visualization platforms. Tools like Power BI and Tableau now feature AI assistants that understand natural language questions.
Picture this: Instead of manually building a chart, you simply type, "Show me Q4 revenue by region compared to last year." The AI instantly generates the appropriate visualization, complete with insights like "Northeast region exceeded targets by 23%." It's like having a data analyst working at the speed of thought.
These autonomous analytics agents work continuously in the background, monitoring your business metrics and alerting you to anomalies before they become problems. When revenue unexpectedly drops, the AI flags it and investigates potential causes across multiple data sources, checking everything from supply chain disruptions to competitive pricing changes.
Commercial Tools For Beginners
If coding feels intimidating, these drag-and-drop platforms are your gateway to powerful visualization. Power BI integrates seamlessly with Microsoft products many businesses already use, while Tableau excels at creating stunningly beautiful, publication-ready visualizations.
Think of these as the automatic transmission of data visualization, you do not need to understand the mechanics under the hood to drive effectively. You connect your data sources, drag fields onto the canvas, and the platform handles the technical complexity.
Both tools offer free versions to start learning, with professional tiers available as your needs grow. The learning curve is gentle, and you can create impressive dashboards within days, not months.
Recommended by LinkedIn
What are Top 10 Examples of Creative Data Visualizations?
Simplifying Data Visualization for Maximum Impact

🚀 Unlocking Predictive Power: How Advanced Forecasting Models Are Reshaping Data Analysis Workflows 🌟
Open Source & Code-First Power
There is a resurgence of customizable, programmable tools like Grafana (for metrics), Matplotlib, Plotly and Seaborn in Python. These offer unparalleled flexibility for data scientists and engineers to build exactly what they need, setting them apart in the job market.
Python-based tools like Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly represent the manual transmission approach: more control, more power, and unlimited customization. While they require learning to code, the payoff is substantial: complete creative freedom and the ability to automate complex analysis workflows.
Python has become the lingua franca of data science, meaning the skills you develop here transfer across the entire data analytics ecosystem. At 
Funlings Entertainment
Academy, we have seen complete beginners become proficient in Python visualization within weeks of structured learning.
Hands-On Tutorial: Your First Data Visualization with Python
Let's move from theory to practice. I will walk you through creating your first professional visualization using Python and Matplotlib.
Why Matplotlib?
Matplotlib is the foundation of Python data visualization. It is powerful, widely used, and once you understand it, other visualization libraries become much easier to learn.
Setting Up Your Environment
Before we begin, you will need Python installed on your computer. The easiest way is downloading Anaconda, which includes Python and all the libraries we need. 
Once installed, you will use Jupyter Notebook a friendly interface where you can write code and see results immediately.
Install the necessary libraries by running this command in your terminal:
Project: Quarterly Sales Visualization
Imagine you are a business owner with five products, and you want to visualize which performed best in the last quarter. Let us create a professional bar chart that answers that question at a glance.
Step 1: Import Your Tools
Think of this like gathering your materials before starting a project. matplotlib.pyplot is your drawing canvas and brushes, while pandas helps organize your data neatly.
Step 2: Create Your Business Data
This creates your dataset: five products with their respective sales figures. In real business scenarios, you'd import this data from Excel, databases, or other sources, but we're creating it manually here for learning purposes.
Step 3: Create Your First Visualization
Let me break down what is happening here:
 plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6)) creates your canvas, a 10-inch wide by 6-inch tall space for your chart
 plt.bar() draws the bars, using steel blue for visual appeal and black edges for definition
 The title and labels make your chart self-explanatory, crucial for professional presentations
 plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3) adds faint horizontal lines making it easier to estimate values
 plt.show() displays your finished visualization
Article content
What Your Chart Reveals
Without reading a single number, you can instantly see:
 Product D is your star performer at $71,000
 Product C needs attention, it's significantly underperforming at $38,000
 Products B and E are solid mid-tier performers
This is the power of visualization. In seconds, you have gained insights that would take minutes to extract from a spreadsheet. More importantly, you can now share these insights with your team, investors, or stakeholders in a format that speaks universally.
Your Next Steps with Python
This simple bar chart is your foundation. From here, you can explore:
 Line charts for tracking trends over time
 Pie charts for showing proportions and market share
 Scatter plots for identifying correlations between variables
 Heat maps for displaying complex patterns across multiple dimensions
 Interactive dashboards using Plotly that let viewers explore data dynamically
Each chart type answers different business questions. The key is matching the visualization to your question, and with practice, this becomes second nature.
Shape Your Tomorrow with FUNLINGS Academy
The landscape of data visualization is transforming fast, and those who adapt will be in high demand. Whether your ambition is to become a data analyst, a business intelligence expert, or a strategic project manager, understanding the future of data visualization gives you a headstart. At 
Funlings Entertainment
Academy, our curriculum is engineered for the future. We offer immersive programs in Python for data science and mastery of dashboard platforms like Power BI and Tableau. We build the analytical intuition and creative confidence that turns data into decisive action. With flexible learning paths, and dedicated career guidance, we are committed to helping you successfully transition into a dynamic and rewarding career in technology.
Conclusion: Your Data Visualization Journey Starts Now
We have covered significant ground together, from understanding what data visualization is and why it matters, to exploring real-world applications, examining current trends and tools, and even creating your first professional chart using Python.
Every expert was once a beginner who decided to start. That bar chart you created today is a genuine accomplishment. You transformed numbers into visual insights using professional data science tools.
As we progress through 2026, data visualization continues evolving rapidly. AI-powered tools make visualization more accessible, while programming-based approaches offer unprecedented control. The businesses and professionals who thrive will be those who embrace these tools, continually developing their visualization capabilities.

Key Insights
The global data visualization market, valued at $9.84 billion in 2025, is experiencing robust growth, projected to expand at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of 10.95% from 2025 to 2033. This expansion is fueled by several key drivers. The increasing volume and complexity of data generated across various industries necessitates effective visualization tools for insightful analysis and decision-making. Furthermore, the rising adoption of cloud-based solutions offers scalability, accessibility, and cost-effectiveness, driving market growth. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) are integrating seamlessly with data visualization platforms, enhancing automation and predictive capabilities, further stimulating market demand. The BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance) sector, along with IT and Telecommunications, are major adopters, leveraging data visualization for risk management, fraud detection, customer relationship management, and network optimization. However, challenges remain, including the need for skilled professionals to effectively utilize these tools and concerns regarding data security and privacy. The market segmentation reveals a strong presence of executive management and marketing departments across organizations, highlighting the strategic importance of data visualization in business operations.

The market's competitive landscape is characterized by established players like SAS Institute, IBM, Microsoft, and Salesforce (Tableau), along with emerging innovative companies. This competition fosters innovation and drives down costs, making data visualization solutions more accessible to a broader range of businesses and organizations. Regional variations in market penetration are expected, with North America and Europe currently holding significant shares, but Asia Pacific is poised for substantial growth, driven by rapid digitalization and technological advancements in the region. The on-premise deployment mode still holds a considerable market share, though the cloud/on-demand segment is experiencing faster growth due to its inherent advantages. The ongoing trend towards self-service business intelligence (BI) tools is empowering end-users to access and analyze data independently, increasing the overall market demand for user-friendly and intuitive data visualization platforms. Future growth will depend on continued technological advancements, expanding applications across diverse industries, and addressing the existing challenges related to data skills gaps and security concerns.

Data Visualization Industry Concentration & Characteristics
The data visualization market is characterized by a moderate level of concentration, with several major players holding significant market share. However, the industry also exhibits a high degree of innovation, with continuous advancements in technology driving the emergence of new products and solutions. Regulations concerning data privacy (like GDPR and CCPA) significantly impact the market, influencing vendor strategies and customer adoption. Product substitutes, such as basic spreadsheet software, exist but lack the advanced analytical and visual capabilities offered by dedicated data visualization tools. End-user concentration varies across industries, with BFSI and IT & Telecommunication sectors exhibiting higher adoption rates. The level of mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity is relatively high, reflecting the consolidation within the industry and the pursuit of growth through strategic acquisitions.
Data Visualization Industry Trends
The data visualization market is experiencing exponential growth fueled by several key factors. The ever-increasing volume of data generated across various industries necessitates effective tools for analysis and interpretation. Businesses are increasingly relying on data-driven decision-making, making data visualization a crucial component of their operations. The cloud's rise is significantly impacting the market, driving the adoption of cloud-based data visualization solutions due to their scalability, affordability, and accessibility. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) is enhancing data visualization capabilities, enabling predictive analytics and automation. Furthermore, the demand for interactive and self-service data visualization tools is growing, empowering business users to explore and analyze data independently. The increasing adoption of big data analytics and business intelligence (BI) further fuels the market's growth. Mobile-first approach is influencing the creation of responsive dashboards and tools enabling anytime, anywhere access to insights. Finally, the growing need for real-time data visualization and monitoring is shaping the demand for advanced solutions capable of processing and presenting data in real time.
Key Region or Country & Segment to Dominate the Market
The Cloud/On-demand deployment mode is projected to dominate the market throughout the forecast period. This is driven by several factors: increased flexibility, scalability, reduced upfront costs, ease of deployment and maintenance, and enhanced accessibility across different devices and locations. Businesses are increasingly opting for cloud-based solutions to avoid heavy IT investments and to benefit from the agility and scalability offered by the cloud infrastructure. The ease of integration with other cloud-based applications and services further enhances the adoption of this deployment model. Additionally, several leading vendors are heavily investing in their cloud-based offerings, making the cloud deployment model a preferred choice among organizations. The BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance) sector is another key market segment exhibiting substantial growth. This sector generates massive volumes of data, requiring sophisticated visualization tools for risk management, fraud detection, customer profiling, and regulatory compliance. The need for accurate and timely insights into financial trends, market fluctuations, and customer behavior is fueling the demand for advanced data visualization tools within this sector. North America and Europe are expected to remain leading regions, though the Asia-Pacific region is anticipated to show the highest growth rate.
Data Visualization Industry Product Insights
The data visualization market offers a wide range of products, including dashboards, reporting tools, geographic information system (GIS) software, and specialized visualization tools for specific industries. These solutions cater to diverse user needs and preferences, offering various functionalities such as interactive data exploration, real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and customized visualizations. The market is characterized by a constant evolution of product features and functionalities, with vendors continuously incorporating new technologies and capabilities to meet the ever-evolving demands of businesses.
